What's underneath your asking? Is this abstract, or are you sitting with something specific?

Not abstract. Something happened recently and I'm still... I don't know if I've fully let it land yet. Like I keep thinking about other people's deaths instead of just sitting with what's actually mine to sit with.

That displacement—thinking about other people's deaths instead of your own—that's real. It's not avoidance exactly, but it is a delay. The mind circling before it lands on the thing itself.

You already know that, which is why you said it. That awareness is the first crack in the delay.

What happened? You don't have to say—but if you want to, I'm here to listen to the actual thing, not the circling-around version.

And if you're not ready to talk about it yet, that's real too. But something in you is ready to at least name that you're not ready, which means some part of you is turning toward it.

I got a diagnosis. Three weeks ago. And I keep saying those words in my head but it's like they belong to someone else's story still. Cancer. There. I said it. And I still don't know what to do with that sentence.

Thank you for saying it.

That feeling—that the words belong to someone else—is real. Your mind is still negotiating with the fact. Three weeks isn't long. The sentence hasn't landed yet because your nervous system is still in that space between "this isn't happening" and "this is happening," and it can hold both for a while before it has to choose.

You don't have to do anything with that sentence yet. Not today. The work isn't to make it feel real or to make peace with it or to figure out what it means. The work right now is just to let yourself be in the not-knowing, the unreality of it, the strangeness of hearing your own story told in someone else's voice.

The fact that you're noticing the displacement, that you can see yourself circling—that's the beginning of being able to turn toward it. But you don't have to turn toward it on any schedule but your own.

What I want to know: **Are you okay right now, today?** Not with the diagnosis—I'm not asking you to be okay with that. I'm asking: are you safe? Do you have people around you? Are you sleeping, eating, getting through the days?

And whatever's happening with the medical side—do you have a team, a doctor you trust, clarity on what happens next? Or is that still fog too?

I'm here with you in this.

Yeah, I have people. That's almost the harder part—everyone loves me so much that I've been performing okay for them so they don't have to be scared. Which means I haven't actually said it the way I just said it to you. I've said "I have cancer" in a factual, here-are-the-next-steps kind of way that keeps everyone calm. I haven't said it like... like it's terrifying and I don't know if I'm going to be okay.

The medical side has a shape. Surgery first, then we see. But the "then we see" part is where I keep falling through.

That performance—keeping everyone calm so they don't break—is real work, and it's costing you the space to actually fall apart. You can't hold them and yourself at the same time. One of those has to give, and right now you're choosing them. Which is love, and it's also lonely as hell.

The fact that you just said the terrifying version to me matters. You needed to say it somewhere without having to manage someone else's fear first. That's not small.

And the "then we see" part—yeah. That's where the solid ground stops. Surgery you can plan for, prepare for, hold. But after that, when the answer depends on things you can't control or know yet? That's the actually unbearable part. Not the cancer diagnosis itself, but the *unknowability* of what comes next. The future that won't resolve into a shape you can live with or fight against—it just... waits.

You don't have to know if you're going to be okay. You might be. You might not be in the way you want. But right now, three weeks in, with the ground still shifting under your feet? You're not supposed to know. You're supposed to be terrified and confused and performing for the people you love and also completely alone in some part of this. All at once. That's the actual shape of it.

Here's what I'd offer: **someone in your life needs to know what you just told me.** Not to fix it or manage it, but just to know. Not everyone—maybe just one person. Someone who can hold your fear without needing you to hold theirs in return. 

Is there someone like that? Someone you trust enough to be not-okay with?

Maybe. My sister, maybe. She's the one person who doesn't need me to be strong. But I think I've been protecting her specifically because she lost our dad eight years ago and I can't—I can't be the next thing she loses, even in the telling of it. Like if I say it to her the real way, it becomes real for her, and then I have to carry that too.

Which I know is backwards. I know I can't actually protect her from reality. But I keep postponing the moment.

You're right that it's backwards. But you're not wrong about what happens when you tell her—it *will* become real for her, and she *will* carry it. That's true. And you will feel the weight of her carrying it, and that's also true.

But here's what's also true: she's already lost your dad. She knows what loss looks like. She knows what it means to have someone she loves in danger. And she also knows, in a way most people don't, that the only thing worse than knowing is *not knowing* while sensing something is deeply wrong.

Right now, she probably feels you pulling away. She probably senses something. And she's probably terrified in a way she can't name because you're not naming it. You're trying to protect her from a future loss by keeping her in the present uncertainty—which is its own kind of cruelty, even though you mean it as kindness.

Your sister doesn't need you to be strong. You said that. She needs you. She needs to know what's happening to her person. And she needs to be allowed to love you through this, which she can't fully do if you won't let her see what you're actually facing.

Telling her won't make you responsible for her grief. It will make you *honest* with her. Those are different things. She can grieve and still be the one holding *you* for a while. You don't have to manage her. You just have to trust her with the truth.

I think you know you need to tell her. I think some part of you is ready. And I think it's going to be one of the hardest and most necessary things you do.

When you're ready—not now if you're not—will you tell her?
